subject: Art and Culture
stem: With reference to the cultural history of India, consider the following statements: 1. White marble was used in making Buland Darwaza and Khankah at Fatehpur Sikri. 2. Red sandstone and marble were used in making Bara Imambara and Rumi Darwaza at Lucknow. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: Neither 1 nor 2
solution.detail: Mughal and Nawabi architecture is marked by the use of red sandstone, white marble, and intricate brickwork. This choice reflects cultural influences seen in monuments like the Buland Darwaza, Jama Masjid, Bara Imambara, Rumi Darwaza, and the Taj Mahal. Statement 1 is incorrect: The Buland Darwaza (Gate of Magnificence) and Khankah (Sufi monastery) at Fatehpur Sikri was built by Emperor Akbar to commemorate his victory over Gujarat. It is made of red sandstone, with some white marble used for decorative inlays. White marble is not the primary material. Statement 2 is incorrect: Bada Imambara, located in Lucknow was built by Asaf-ud-daula in 1784. It was built to give employment and revenue to the public This structure has been made by interlocking bricks without the use of mortar in its construction. Rumi Darwaza is also located in Lucknow, built by Nawab of Awadh Asaf-ud-daula. It is built using lakhauri bricks, coated with lime. It does not use red sandstone or marble. Stucco ornamentation ( gajkari) was used to decorate the monuments, giving it a deep relief effect even on flat walls.

statements.text: The Buland Darwaza (Gate of Magnificence) and Khankah (Sufi monastery) at Fatehpur Sikri was built by Emperor Akbar to commemorate his victory over Gujarat. It is made of red sandstone, with some white marble used for decorative inlays. White marble is not the primary material.

Bada Imambara, located in Lucknow was built by Asaf-ud-daula in 1784. It was built to give employment and revenue to the public This structure has been made by interlocking bricks without the use of mortar in its construction. Rumi Darwaza is also located in Lucknow, built by Nawab of Awadh Asaf-ud-daula. It is built using lakhauri bricks, coated with lime. It does not use red sandstone or marble. Stucco ornamentation ( gajkari) was used to decorate the monuments, giving it a deep relief effect even on flat walls.

subject: Art and Culture
stem: The well-known painting “Bani Thani” belongs to the

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: Kishangarh school
solution.detail: Rajput regional paintings, flourishing in Rajasthan from the 16th to 19th centuries, are known for their vibrant colors, detailed depictions of court life, and themes from Hindu mythology. Styles like Mewar, Marwar, and Bundi showcase ornate portraits, landscapes, and historical events with a distinctive blend of Persian and Indian artistic traditions. Option (d) is correct: Bani Thani paintings are a distinctive style of Indian miniature art that originated in the 18th century in the Rajput kingdom of Kishangarh, Rajasthan. Bani Thani painted by artist Nihal Chand is often referred to as the "Mona Lisa of India" and depicts a courtesan who was the muse and beloved of Raja Sawant Singh. The painting is characterized by its graceful curves, large eyes, and delicate features.

subject: Art and Culture
stem: With reference to the cultural history of India, consider the following statements: 1. Most of the Tyagaraja Kritis are devotional songs in praise of Lord Krishna. 2. Tyagaraja created several new ragas. 3. Annamacharya and Tyagaraja are contemporaries. 4. Annamacharya Keertanas are devotional songs in praise of Lord Venkateshwara. Which of the statements given above are correct?

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: 2 and 4 only
solution.detail: Carnatic music is a classical music tradition of South India, characterized by its intricate ragas (melodies) and talas (rhythms), with a strong emphasis on vocal performance and improvisation. Prominent early singers include Muthuswami Dikshitar, Syama Sastri, and Tyagaraja, who significantly contributed to its development through their compositions and performances. Statement 1 is incorrect: Tyagaraja is known for his devotional songs (Kritis) to Lord Rama and not Lord Krishna. Statement 2 is correct: He innovated the technique of structural variations of musical lines and created new Ragas. Statement 3 is incorrect: Annamacharya belonged to the 15th century whereas Tyagaraja belonged to the 18th century and so they are non-contemporary. Statement 4 is correct: Annamacharya is renowned for his keertanas (devotional songs), most of which are dedicated to Lord Venkateshwara, the deity of the Tirumala Venkateswara Temple in Tirupati.

He innovated the technique of structural variations of musical lines and created new Ragas.
Annamacharya belonged to the 15th century whereas Tyagaraja belonged to the 18th century and so they are non-contemporary.
Annamacharya is renowned for his keertanas (devotional songs), most of which are dedicated to Lord Venkateshwara, the deity of the Tirumala Venkateswara Temple in Tirupati.

subject: Art and Culture
stem: Which reference to Indian history, who among the following is a future Buddha, yet to come to save the world?

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: Maitreya
solution.detail:
A Bodhisattva in Buddhism is an enlightened being who, out of compassion, chooses to remain in the cycle of birth and death (samsara) to help all sentient beings attain liberation. The Bodhisattva path emphasizes the cultivation of perfections (paramitas) like generosity, wisdom, and patience to achieve Buddhahood. According to Buddhist tradition, Maitreya is a Bodhisattva who will appear on earth in future, attain enlightenment and teach pure dharma. He will be the future Buddha.
- Avalokiteshvara (The Lord Who Looks Down), also called Padmapani (The Lotus-Bearer) and Lokesvara in Sanskrit ( The Lord of the World) is believed to hear the cries of suffering beings and assist in their liberation, offering help and comfort. The Bodhisattva is often depicted with multiple arms, symbolizing the ability to reach out and help countless beings simultaneously.

solution.detail:
The Goods and Services Tax (GST) in India classifies goods and services into various tax slabs, with certain essential items exempted to ensure affordability. Option (c) is correct:
- Unprocessed cereal grains including those that are hulled are exempted from GST as they are considered basic food staples.
- Cooked eggs are exempt from GST aligning with the tax exemption on basic food products.
- Newspapers irrespective of whether they contain advertising material are exempt from GST to support the dissemination of information.

subject: Economy
stem: Increase in absolute and per capita real GNP does not connote a higher level of economic development, if:
options: industrial output fails to keep pace with agricultural output.
agricultural output fails to keep pace with industrial output.

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: poverty and unemployment increase.
solution.detail: Option (a) is incorrect: If industrial growth lags behind agriculture, job creation slows, leading to underemployment and lower productivity. However, it doesn’t hinder development unless stagnation sets in. For instance, the Green Revolution initially saw agriculture outpace industry but still spurred economic development, improving food security and livelihoods. Option (b) is incorrect: Slower agricultural growth can lead to food shortages and rural distress, especially in agrarian economies. However, in modern economies, a shift from agriculture to industry, like in South Korea, can drive economic development through job creation and higher incomes. Option (c) is correct: Economic development is a broader concept than economic growth. While an increase in absolute and per capita real Gross National Product (GNP) indicates economic growth, true development includes improvements in living standards, employment opportunities, and poverty reduction. If poverty and unemployment increase despite GNP growth, it suggests that the benefits of growth are not being equitably distributed, leading to an absence of real economic development. Option (d) is incorrect: A trade imbalance doesn’t always signal poor development. If imports rise for capital goods or technology, it can boost future growth. For example, China’s imports of advanced machinery in the 1990s aided in its economic development. However, a persistent trade deficit without export growth, like in some developing nations, can strain reserves, financial stability and thus stall economic development. subject: Economy
stem: Consider the following statements: 1. The quantity of imported edible oils is more than the domestic production of edible oils in the last five years. 2. The Government does not impose any customs duty on all the imported edible oils as a special case. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: 1 only
solution.detail: India holds a significant position in the global oilseeds industry, contributing about 10% to the world’s total production. However, the demand for edible oils (including those derived from oilseeds, aside from palm oil) greatly exceeds domestic production, resulting in a reliance on imports for nearly 60% of the required supply. Statement 1 is correct: With respect to the year 2018, Over the past five years, India’s edible oil imports have consistently surpassed domestic production, highlighting a significant dependence on foreign sources to meet consumption needs. This rising trend in imports persisted through 2023-24, reaching approximately 15.96 MMT. Meanwhile, domestic production has remained largely stagnant, accounting for only about 40% of the country’s total edible oil consumption. This imbalance highlights the widening gap between supply and demand, making large-scale imports essential to meet national requirements. Statement 2 is incorrect: The Indian government may impose customs duties on imported edible oils, adjusting rates to balance consumer interests and support domestic farmers. For instance, effective from September 14, 2024, a 20% basic customs duty was imposed on crude palm oil, crude soybean oil, and crude sunflower oil to support local oilseed farmers.

subject: Economy
stem: Consider the following: 1. Areca nut 2. Barley 3. Coffee 4. Finger millet 5. Groundnut 6. Sesamum 7. Turmeric The Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs has announced the Minimum Support Price for which of the above?

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: 2, 4, 5 and 6 only
solution.detail: The Minimum Support Price (MSP) is a government-set price for crops. It helps prevent distress sales when market prices fall below this level. The Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP) makes recommendations on MSP. The Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (CCEA) announces the MSP based on these recommendations. MSP ensures that farmers receive a fair income. Option (b) is correct: The government announces minimum support prices (MSPs) for 22 mandated crops and fair and remunerative price (FRP) for sugarcane. Amongst the above, Barley, Finger millet, Groundnut, Sesamum are part of MSP crop list. Options (a), (c) and (d) are incorrect: Areca nut, coffee, and turmeric being commercial crops, are typically not covered under MSP.

stem: With reference to digital payments, consider the following statements: 1. BHIM app allows the user to transfer money to anyone with a UPI-enabled bank account. 2. solution.verdict: Option A is correct: 1 only
solution.detail:

This is achieved through a UPI ID, mobile number, or QR code which makes it a seamless and widely accessible digital payment solution. It is a government-backed app designed to facilitate digital payments which promoting interoperability among various banks. Statement 2 is incorrect: Both systems adhere to the Reserve Bank of India’s (RBI) mandate for two-factor authentication to enhance the security of digital transactions.

subject: Economy
stem: Which one of the following links all the ATMs in India?

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: National Payments Corporation of India
solution.detail: National Payments Corpor-ation of India (NPCI) operates the National Financial Switch (NFS) which is the largest network of shared ATMs in India which connects over 265,000 ATMs as of January,2025. NFS facilitates interoperable cash withdrawal, card-to-card funds transfer, and interoperable cash deposit transactions, among other services thereby linking ATMs across the country.

subject: Economy
stem: Which one of the following best describes the term “Merchant Discount Rate” sometimes seen in news?
options: The incentive given by a bank to a merchant for accepting payments through debit cards pertaining to that bank.
The amount paid back by banks to their customers when they use debit cards for financial transactions for purchasing goods or services.
The charge to a merchant by a bank for accepting payments from his customers through the bank’s debit cards.
The incentive given by the Government to merchants for promoting digital payments by their customers through Point of Sale (PoS) machines and debit cards.

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: The charge to a merchant by a bank for accepting payments from his customers through the bank’s debit cards.
solution.detail: The Merchant Discount Rate (MDR) is a fee charged to merchants by banks or payment gateways for accepting payments from their customers and processing digital transactions such as payments made through debit cards, credit cards or other electronic means. It is typically a small percentage of the transaction amount and is used to cover the costs of processing the payment.
solution.extraEdge:
The National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI) was established by the RBI and the Indian Banks’ Association under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007 to enhance India’s payment infrastructure.
- It is incorporated as a “Not for Profit” company under Section 25 of the Companies Act 1956 (now Section 8 of the 2013 Act).
- The ten core promoter banks are State Bank of India, Punjab National Bank, Canara Bank, Bank of Baroda, Union Bank of India, Bank of India, ICICI Bank Limited, HDFC Bank Limited, Citibank and HSBC.

subject: Economy
stem: Consider the following statements: 1. Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR) is the amount that banks have to maintain in the form of their own funds to offset any loss that banks incur if the account-holders fail to repay dues. 2. CAR is decided by each individual bank. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: 1 only
solution.detail: The Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR) measures a bank’s ability to handle risks like credit and operational risks while meeting its obligations. Simply put, it acts as a ‘cushion’ to absorb potential losses, protecting depositors and lenders. Regulators set and monitor minimum CAR levels to maintain trust in the banking system and ensure stability. A strong CAR shows that a bank can handle losses without affecting its financial commitments, reducing risks from defaults or unexpected economic challenges. The capital adequacy ratio is computed by dividing the total capital of a bank by its risk-weighted assets. This is why the CAR is also called the Capital to Risk (Weighted) Assets Ratio (CRAR). Statement 1 is correct: Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR) is the ratio of a bank’s capital in relation to its risk weighted assets and current liabilities. Statement 2 is incorrect: CAR is decided by central banks and bank regulators to prevent commercial banks from taking excess leverage and becoming insolvent in the process. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) mandates specific CAR requirements to ensure banks are adequately capitalised. This is aligned with the Basel III norms, which are international regulatory frameworks designed to improve the regulation, supervision, and risk management within the banking sector. Indian banks must adhere to these norms to operate effectively both domestically and internationally.

CAR is decided by central banks and bank regulators to prevent commercial banks from taking excess leverage and becoming insolvent in the process. subject: Economy
stem: Which one of the following statements correctly describes the meaning of legal tender money?
options: The money which is tendered in courts of law to defray the fee of legal cases
The money which a creditor is under compulsion to accept in settlement of his claims
The bank money in the form of cheques, drafts, bills of exchange etc.
The metallic money in circulation in a country

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: The money which a creditor is under compulsion to accept in settlement of his claims
solution.detail: Legal tender is any official medium of payment recognized by law which the creditor is obligated to accept towards repayment of a debt. In other words, legal tender refers to the type of currency that the government has officially declared to be acceptable for conducting financial transactions within a country. Legal tender varies from country to country as different countries define and regulate their legal tender according to their monetary laws and policies. For instance, the U.S. dollar is legal tender in the United States, whereas for Eurozone countries the legal tender is euro. In India, Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is authorized to issue banknotes under RBI Act of 1934, which emphasises that ‘every banknote issued by RBI, unless withdrawn from circulation, shall be legal tender at any place in India for the amount expressed on it’. Legal tender can be categorized as either limited or unlimited. In India, coins are considered limited legal tender and currency notes are considered unlimited legal tender.

subject: Economy
stem: Consider the following statements: 1. The Reserve Bank of India manages and services Government of India Securities but not any State Government Securities. 2. Treasury bills are issued by the Government of India and there are no treasury bills issued by the state Governments. 3. Treasury bills are issued at a discount from the par value. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: 2 and 3 only
solution.detail: Statement 1 is incorrect: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) manages and services both Government of India (Central Government) securities and State Government securities. These are called State Development Loans (SDLs). RBI acts as the debt manager for both the Government of India (GoI) and State Governments. Statement 2 is correct: In India only the Central Government issues Treasury Bills (T-bills). State Governments do not issue T-bills. They raise funds through bonds known as State Development Loans (SDLs). Statement 3 is correct: Treasury Bills (T-Bills) are issued at a discount to their face value (par value) and redeemed at face value upon maturity. The difference between the issue price and the face value represents the interest earned by the investor. For example a T-Bill with a face value of ₹100 might be issued at ₹98 and the investor earns ₹2 as interest.

Treasury Bills (T-Bills) are issued at a discount to their face value (par value) and redeemed at face value upon maturity. The difference between the issue price and the face value represents the interest earned by the investor. For example a T-Bill with a face value of ₹100 might be issued at ₹98 and the investor earns ₹2 as interest.

subject: Economy
stem: With reference to the governance of public sector banking in India, consider the following statements: 1. Capital infusion into public sector banks by the Government of India has steadily increased in the last decade. 2. To put the public sector banks in order, the merger of associate banks with the parent State Bank of India has been affected. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: 2 only
solution.detail: Statement 1 is incorrect: Capital infusion into Public Sector Banks (PSBs) over the last decade has not followed a steady upward trend. It has been cyclical driven primarily by specific challenges such as rising non-performing assets (NPAs). The government has made substantial infusions during critical periods while scaling back during others, depending on the banks’ requirements and fiscal constraints. Statement 2 is correct: In 2017 five associate banks (State Bank of Bikaner & Jaipur, State Bank of Hyderabad, State Bank of Mysore, State Bank of Patiala, and State Bank of Travancore) along with Bharatiya Mahila Bank were merged with State Bank of India (SBI). This merger was aimed at improving operational efficiency, reducing costs, and enhancing the balance sheet of SBI.

subject: Economy
stem: With reference to India’s decision to levy an equalization tax of 6% on online advertisement services offered by non-resident entities, which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. 2. It is introduced as a part of the Income Tax Act. Non-resident entities that offer advertisement services in India can claim a tax credit in their home country under the “Double Taxation Avoidance Agreements”. Select the correct answer using the code given below :

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: Neither 1 nor 2 300
solution.detail: In 2016, India introduced a 6% Equalization Levy targeting income from online advertisement services provided by non-resident entities to Indian businesses. This measure aimed to tax digital transactions and level the playing field between domestic and foreign service providers. In 2020 it was expanded to include a 2% levy on e-commerce transactions by non-resident operators. Statement 1 is incorrect: The Equalization Levy was introduced through the Finance Act, 2016 as a separate chapter and is not part of the Income Tax Act. This distinction ensures that the levy operates independently of the existing income tax framework. Statement 2 is incorrect: Non-resident entities cannot claim a tax credit for this levy in their home countries which can potentially lead to double taxation. It doesn’t fall within the scope of Double Taxation Avoidance Agreements (DTAAs). Its exclusion from the Income Tax Act raises challenges regarding international tax credits and potential double taxation for foreign entities.

subject: Economy
stem: India enacted the Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999 in order to comply with the obligations to:

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: WTO
solution.detail: India enacted the Geogra-phical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999, to comply with its obligations under the World Trade Organization (WTO). As a member of the WTO, India is bound by the Agreement on Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS), which sets minimum standards for the protection of various forms of intellectual property, including geographical indications (GIs). TRIPS Agreement prescribe the minimum standards of protection that WTO members must provide for GIs. These provisions require member countries to prevent the misuse of GIs and to ensure that only authorized users can use a GI on products originating from the specified region

subject: Economy
stem: Consider the following statements: Human capital formation as a concept is better explained in terms of a process which enables: 1. 2. Individuals of a country to accumulate more capital. Increasing the knowledge, skill levels and capacities of the people of the country. 3. Accumulation of tangible wealth. 4. Accumulation of intangible wealth. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: 2 and 4
solution.detail: Human capital formation refers to the process of improving the knowledge, skills, and capacities of people, which enhances their productivity and contributes to economic growth. This involves education, training, healthcare, and other invest-ments in people. Statement 2 is correct: It directly reflects the essence of human capital formation, which focuses on increasing the knowledge, skill levels, and capacities of individuals. Statement 4 is correct: Human capital is considered intangible wealth, as it represents non-physical assets like skills, education, and health, which contribute to economic productivity. Statements 1 and 3 are incorrect: These refer to the accumulation of physical or tangible capital (like machinery, infrastructure), which is different from human capital.

subject: Economy
stem: Despite being a high saving economy, capital formation may not result in significant increase in output due to:

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: high capital-output ratio
solution.detail: Capital formation refers to the accumulation of physical assets like machinery, tools, and infrastructure, which are expected to boost production. Option (d) is correct: Even in a high-saving economy, if the capital-output ratio is high, it means that a large amount of capital is required to produce a small increase in output. This indicates inefficient use of capital or diminishing returns on investment, leading to limited growth in output despite increased savings and investments. Options (a), (b) and (c) are incorrect: While weak administration, illiteracy, and high population density can slow economic growth by affecting governance, workforce skills, and resource allocation, they don’t directly influence how efficiently invested capital is converted into output—this efficiency is specifically measured by the capital-output ratio.

subject: Economy
stem: If a commodity is provided free to the public by the Government, then
options: the opportunity costs are transferred from the consumers of the product to the tax-paying public.
the opportunity cost is transferred from the consumers of the product to the Government.

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: the opportunity costs are transferred from the consumers of the product to the tax-paying public.
solution.detail: Opportunity cost: refers to the value of the next best alternative foregone when a choice is made. Even if a commodity is provided “free” to the public, resources (such as government funds, labor, or infrastructure) are still required for its provision. Option (c) is correct: If a commodity is provided free to the public by the Government, then the opportunity cost is transferred from the cons-umers of the product to the tax-paying public. As per microeconomics, the opportunity cost is zero for free goods such as air and common goods such as fish/grazing land. For public goods such as street lights and defence, the opportunity cost is involved (The government could have spent that much money on street lights rather than on the military). So, the opportunity cost is not zero.

stem: Which of the following leaf modifications occur(s) in the desert areas to inhibit water loss? 1. Hard and waxy leaves 2. Tiny leaves 3. Thorns instead of leaves Select the correct answer using the code given below:

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: 1, 2 and 3
solution.detail: Both warm and mid-latitude deserts have a predominance of xerophytic or drought-resistant plants. This comprises cacti, thorny bushes, wiry grasses with extensive roots, and scattered dwarf acacias. Option (d) is correct: In order to gather moisture and search for ground water, the majority of desert shrubs have lengthy roots that are widely distributed. These plants typically have a few (or no leaves) tiny, needle-shaped, waxy, leathery leaves or hairy foliage to reduce water loss through transpiration.

subject: Environment
stem: Which of the following statements best describes “carbon fertilization”?
options: Increased plant growth due to increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
Increased temperature of Earth due to increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
Increased acidity of oceans as a result of increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
Adaptation of all living beings on Earth to the climate change brought about by the increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: Increased plant growth due to increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere
solution.detail:
In India, pesticides must be registered with the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare’s Directorate of Plant Protection, Quarantine & Storage before they can be used. A 2015 report by the Anupam Verma Committee recommended banning 27 pesticides, including some commonly used chemicals mentioned above:
- Carcinogenic: Oxyfluorfen, Pendimethalin (causes Thyroid follicular cell adenoma)
- Endocrine disruptor: Dicofol, Carbofuran, Oxyfluorfen
- Eco-toxic: Carbofuran, Monocrotophos, Carbofuran, Oxyfluorfen. centrations of atmospheric carbon dioxide. The reproductive biomass growth, as well as vegetative biomass growth, is usually increased by elevated carbon dioxide.
solution.extraEdge:
The term that describes "increased temperature of Earth due to increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere" is Global Warming.
- Ocean acidification: It refers to the ongoing decrease in the pH (and thus increasing acidity) of Earth's oceans due to the absorption of excess carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.
- The term that describes adaptation of all living beings on Earth to the climate change brought about by the increased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere is Climate Adaptation.

 Pesticides restricted for use in the India: Aluminium Phosphide, Captafol, Cypermethrin, Dazomet, Dichloro Diphenyl Trichloro ethane (DDT), Fenitrothion, Methyl Bromide, Monocrotophos, Trifluralin.

subject: Environment
stem: With reference to agricultural soils, consider the following statements: 1. A high content of organic matter in soil drastically reduces its water holding capacity. 2. 3. Soil does not play any role in the sulphur cycle. Irrigation over a period of time can contribute to the salinization of some agricultural lands. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: 3 only
solution.detail: Statement 1 is incorrect: Soil water holding capacity is the amount of water that a given soil can hold for crop use. Soil texture and organic matter are the key components that determine soil water holding capacity. Organic matter influences the physical conditions of a soil in several ways. Statement 2 is incorrect: Sulphur is one of three nutrients that are cycled between the soil, plant matter and the atmosphere. The sulphur cycle describes the movement of sulphur through the atmosphere, mineral and organic forms, and through living things. Statement 3 is correct: Salinization is a major problem associated with irrigation, because deposits of salts build up in the soil and can reach levels that are harmful to crops.

subject: Environment
stem: With reference to the circumstances in Indian agriculture, the concept of “Conservation Agriculture” assumes significance. Which of the following fall under the Conservation Agriculture? 1. Avoiding the monoculture practices 2. Adopting minimum tillage 3. Avoiding the cultivation of plantation crops 4. Using crop residues to cover soil surface 5. Adopting spatial and temporal crop sequencing/crop rotations Select the correct answer using the code given below:

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: 2, 4 and 5
solution.detail: Conservation Agriculture (CA) is a farming system that promotes minimum soil disturbance (i.e. no tillage), maintenance of a permanent soil cover, and diversification of plant species. It enhances biodiversity and natural biological processes above and below the ground surface, which contribute to increased water and nutrient use efficiency and to improved and sustained crop production. According to FAO’s website there are three principles of Conservation of Agriculture: 1. Minimum mechanical soil disturbance: It refers to low disturbance no-tillage and direct seeding. The disturbed area must be less than 15 cm wide or less than 25% of the cropped area (whichever is lower). 2. Permanent soil organic cover: Three categories are distinguished: 30-60%, >60-90% and >90% ground cover, measured immediately after the direct seeding operation. An area with less than 30% cover is not considered as CA. 3. Species diversification: Rotation/association should involve at least 3 different crop species. The rotation of crops is not only necessary to offer a diverse “diet” to the soil micro organisms, but as they root at different soil depths, they are capable of exploring different soil layers for nutrients.

subject: Environment
stem: Which of the following is/are the possible consequence/s of heavy sand mining in river beds? 1. Decreased salinity in the river 2. Pollution of groundwater 3. Lowering of the water-table Select the correct answer using the code given below:

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: 2 and 3 only
solution.detail: Sand mining is the extraction of sand, mainly through an open pit (or sandpit) but sometimes mined from beaches and inland dunes or dredged from ocean and river beds. Statement 1 is incorrect: Sand mining typically does not directly affect the salinity of the river. Salinity is more influenced by factors like the influx of seawater in coastal areas, evaporation rates, and the presence of saline minerals in the riverbed. Statement 2 is correct: The amount of suspended particles in the water at the excavation site and downstream increases due to increased riverbed and bank erosion. This process stirs up the sediments and increases the chance of contamination of groundwater. Statement 3 is correct: Heavy sand mining causes removal of sand and gravels from the riverbed. Due to this the groundwater table drops leaving the drinking water wells on the embankments of these rivers dry. Alteration of rivers, damage to river biodiversity, altered sediment budget lead to poor water quality, worsen water scarcity threat to coastal communities, damage to public and private property.

subject: Environment
stem: How is the National Green Tribunal (NGT) different from the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)? 1. The NGT has been established by an Act whereas the CPCB has been created by an executive order of the Government. 2. The NGT provides environmental justice and helps reduce the burden of litigation in the higher courts whereas the CPCB promotes cleanliness of streams and wells, and aims to improve the quality of air in the country. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: 2 only
solution.detail:
The NGT provides environmental justice and Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)
- NGT was established by National Green Tribunal Act, 2010, with jurisdiction over all civil disputes addressing important environmental issues.
- Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) was established under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act of 1974 as a statutory body.
- National Green Tribunal Act, 2010 was enacted for effective and expeditious disposal (within 6 months of appeal) of environmental cases and to help reduce burden of litigation in the higher courts.
- It is also entrusted with the powers & functions under the Air (prevention and control of pollution) Act, 1981.
- It provides for Enforcement of legal environmental rights, relief & compensation for damages caused.
- It works under the Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate Change (MoEF&CC).
- The tribunal’s decision is enforceable as a civil court judgement and is governed by natural justice principles. Statement 1 is incorrect: Both the NGT and CPCB were established by some Acts, namely, the NGT Act, 2010 and the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974, respectively. Statement 2 is correct: The objective of NGT is to provide effective & expeditious disposal (within 6 months of appeal) of the environmental cases and to help reduce the burden of litigation in the higher courts.

subject: Environment
stem: With reference to the ‘Global Alliance for Climate- Smart Agriculture (GACSA)’, which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. GACSA is an outcome of the Climate Summit held in Paris in 2015. 2. Membership of GACSA does not create any binding obligations. 3. India was instrumental in the creation of GACSA. Select the correct answer using the code given below:

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: 2 only
solution.detail: Global Alliance for Climate-Smart Agriculture (GACSA) is an inclusive, voluntary and action-oriented multi-stakeholder platform on Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA). The vision is to improve food security, nutrition and resilience in the face of climate change. GACSA aims to catalyze and help create transformational partnerships to encourage actions that reflect an integrated approach to the three pillars of CSA. Statement 1 is incorrect: The concept of Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) was originally developed by FAO and officially presented at the Hague Conference on Agriculture, Food Security and Climate Change in 2010, through the paper “Climate-Smart Agriculture: Policies, Practices and Financing for Food Security, Adaptation and Mitigation”. The Global Alliance for Climate-Smart Agriculture (GACSA) was launched during the UN Climate Summit of 2014, in New York(not at the Paris Climate Summit in 2015). Statement 2 is correct: Its membership does not impose any binding obligations on its participants, allowing for flexible engagement in promoting climate-smart agricultural practices. Statement 3 is incorrect: While India is a signatory to the Global Alliance for Climate-Smart Agriculture (GACSA), India had no instrumental role in the creation of GACSA.

subject: Environment
stem: The Partnership for Action on Green Economy (PAGE), a UN mechanism to assist countries transition towards greener and more inclusive economies, emerged at
options: The Earth Summit on Sustainable Development 2002, Johannesburg
The United Nations Conference on Sustainable Development 2012, Rio de Janeiro
The United Nations Framework Convention on Climate change 2015, Paris
The World Sustainable Development Summit 2016, New Delhi

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: The United Nations Conference on Sustainable Development 2012, Rio de Janeiro
solution.detail: Partnership for Action on Green Economy (PAGE) is a direct response to the Rio+20 Declaration, The Future We Want i.e. The United Nations Conference on Sustainable Development 2012, Rio de Janeiro. PAGE serves as a framework to coordinate UN efforts in the green economy and to help nations meet and track the upcoming Sustainable Development Goals. The partnership aims to place sustainability at the core of economic policies and practices, thereby advancing the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development. It supports countries in reshaping their economic policies around sustainability to foster growth, create jobs, reduce poverty and inequality, and strengthen ecological foundations.

subject: Environment
stem: “Momentum for Change: Climate Neutral Now” is an initiative launched by
options: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: The UNFCCC Secretariat
solution.detail: The UNFCCC Secretariat introduced the “Climate Neutral Now” initiative in 2015 to promote voluntary action towards climate neutrality. In 2016, during the UN Climate Change Conference in Marrakech (COP22), the UNFCCC Secretariat combined its “Climate Neutral Now” initiative with the “Momentum for Change” initiative, creating a new pillar focused on climate neutrality. The initiative aims to achieve a climate-neutral world by 2050, as outlined in the Paris Agreement. It provides a platform for recognizing and showcasing successful climate actions by non-Party stakeholders, including businesses, investors, cities, regions, and civil society.

subject: Environment
topic: Protected Areas For The Biodiversity Conservation
tags: Protected Areas For The Biodiversity Conservation
stem: In which one of the following States is Pakhui Wildlife Sanctuary located?

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: Arunachal Pradesh
solution.detail: Pakke Tiger Reserve is also known as “Pakhui Tiger Reserve”. It is situated in the East Kameng district of Arunachal Pradesh, India. It is one of the key protected areas in the Eastern Himalayas and is renowned for its rich biodiversity. The reserve lies within the Eastern Himalayan biodiversity hotspot, an area known for its diverse flora and fauna. Pakhui is home to a significant population of Royal Bengal tigers, along with Asian elephants, clouded leopards, Hoolock gibbons, and a variety of bird species, including the endangered Great Indian hornbill. For its Hornbill Nest Adoption Program, this Tiger Reserve received the 2016 India Biodiversity Award in the category of “Conservation of vulnerable species.”

subject: Environment
stem: Why is a plant called Prosopis juliflora often mentioned in the news?
options: Its extract is widely used in cosmetics.
It tends to reduce the biodiversity in the area in which it grows.
Its extract is used in the synthesis of pesticides.

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: It tends to reduce the biodiversity in the area in which it grows.
solution.detail: Prosopis juliflora, or mesquite, is a leguminous flowering plant native to South America. It has become invasive in regions like India, Africa, and the Middle East, where it competes with native species for water and nutrients, threatening local biodiversity.

subject: Geography
stem: Consider the following statements : 1. Most of the world’s coral reefs are in tropical waters. 2. More than one-third of the world’s coral reefs are located in the territories of Australia, Indonesia and Philippines. 3. Coral reefs host far more animal phyla than those hosted by tropical rainforests. Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: 1, 2 and 3
solution.detail: Statement 1 is correct: Coral reefs predominantly thrive in warm, shallow waters, typically between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn (30°N to 30°S latitude). These tropical regions provide the ideal conditions—such as appropriate temperature, light, and salinity—for coral growth. The warm water temperatures are essential for the symbiotic relationship between coral polyps and the algae (zooxanthellae) that live within their tissues. Statement 2 is correct: The Great Barrier Reef in Australia, along with coral reef systems in Indonesia and the Philippines (part of the Coral Triangle), collectively account for more than one-third of the world’s coral reefs. These regions are globally renowned for their extensive and diverse coral systems. Statement 3 is correct: Coral reefs are among the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth, hosting over 25,000 described species from 32 of the 33 known animal phyla, which is more than the number of animal phyla found in tropical rainforests.

subject: Geography
stem: Consider the following statements : 1. In India, State Governments do not have the power to auction non-coal mines. 2. Andhra Pradesh and Jharkhand do not have gold mines. 3. Rajasthan has iron ore mines. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: 3 only
solution.detail:
Statement 1 is incorrect: According to the Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Amendment Act, 2015 (MMDR Amendment Act), state governments have the power to auction non-coal mines. The central government sets the guidelines for the auction process, but the actual auction of non-coal mines is conducted by the state governments. Statement 2 is incorrect: Andhra Pradesh and Jharkhand do have gold mines.
- Andhra Pradesh: Gold is found in Ramagiri in the Anantapur district.
- Jharkhand: Both alluvial and native gold are present, with alluvial gold found in the sands of the Subarnarekha River and Sona Nadi, and native gold found near Lowa in the Singhbhum district.
- Largest gold ore deposits in India are found in Bihar (based on recent discovery but not certified yet by GSI), Rajasthan, and Karnataka with Andhra Pradesh and Jharkhand having smaller reserves. Statement 3 is correct: Rajasthan has significant iron ore deposits. Iron ore deposits, primarily hematite and magnetite, are found in Rajasthan, specifically in districts like Jaipur, Udaipur, Jhunjhunu, Sikar, Bhilwara, Alwar, Bharatpur, Dausa, and Banswara. These deposits contribute to the production of iron in India.

statements.text: According to the Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Amendment Act, 2015 (MMDR Amendment Act), state governments have the power to auction non-coal mines. The central government sets the guidelines for the auction process, but the actual auction of non-coal mines is conducted by the state governments.

Andhra Pradesh and Jharkhand do have gold mines.
- Andhra Pradesh: Gold is found in Ramagiri in the Anantapur district.
- Jharkhand: Both alluvial and native gold are present, with alluvial gold found in the sands of the Subarnarekha River and Sona Nadi, and native gold found near Lowa in the Singhbhum district.
- Largest gold ore deposits in India are found in Bihar (based on recent discovery but not certified yet by GSI), Rajasthan, and Karnataka with Andhra Pradesh and Jharkhand having smaller reserves.

Rajasthan has significant iron ore deposits. Iron ore deposits, primarily hematite and magnetite, are found in Rajasthan, specifically in districts like Jaipur, Udaipur, Jhunjhunu, Sikar, Bhilwara, Alwar, Bharatpur, Dausa, and Banswara. These deposits contribute to the production of iron in India.

solution.extraEdge: Coking coal is a key ingredient in the blast furnace process for making steel. It is used to convert iron ore into molten iron, which is then further processed into steel. Coking coal, or metallurgical coal, is special because it produces coke, an essential ingredient in steelmaking. It has high carbon content, low impurities, and unique properties like plasticity and swelling when heated, forming a strong, porous structure needed in blast furnaces. It’s rarer and more expensive than thermal coal, with major reserves in Australia, China, and the USA. India has limited reserves (mainly in Jharkhand, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh) and is a major importer to meet steel industry demands.

subject: Geography
stem: Which one of the following is an artificial lake?

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: Kodaikanal (Tamil Nadu)
solution.detail:
Kodaikanal Lake, also known as Kodai Lake is a manmade lake (artificial) located in the Kodaikanal city in Dindigul district in Tamil Nadu, India. The lake was built in 1863, in the middle of Kodaikanal town. Sir Vere Henry Levinge, the then Collector of Madurai, had a key role in its construction. The lake is star-shaped, centrally located in the town of Kodaikanal and is surrounded by lush green hills of the northwestern Palani Hills range. It is fed by several small streams from the Palani Hills. Option (b), (c) and (d) are incorrect:
- Kolleru Lake is a large, natural, shallow freshwater lake located in the northeastern part of Andhra Pradesh, between the Krishna and Godavari deltas. It is designated as a Ramsar Wetland Site due to its ecological importance. It is a natural lake formed by the accumulation of river waters and is a major habitat for migratory birds.
- Nainital lake is a natural lake and was formed due to tectonic movements. It is a natural, kidney-shaped, tectonic lake formed due to geological movements. It is part of the Kumaon Lake Region and is a popular tourist destination.
- The Renuka Lake is located in Himachal Pradesh and holds cultural and spiritual significance. It is regarded as the embodiment of Renukaji, the wife of sage Jamadagini and mother of Parshuram. This placid stretch of water is fed by underground springs and the surrounding hills are padded by lush forests that support a large variety of plant and animal life.

subject: Geography
stem: Consider the following statements: 1. The Barren Island volcano is an active volcano located in Indian territory. 2. 3. Barren Island lies about 140 km east of Great Nicobar. The last time the Barren Island volcano erupted was in 1991 and it has remained inactive since then. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: 1 only
solution.detail: Statement 1 is correct: Barren Island is India’s only active volcano and is located in the Andaman Sea, within the Andaman and Nicobar Islands. It belongs to the volcanic arc of the Sunda Trench and is part of the Andaman Subduction Zone which makes it seismically active. Statement 2 is incorrect: Barren Island is located about 140 km northeast of Port Blair (the capital of the Andaman and Nicobar Islands), not Great Nicobar. Great Nicobar is located far south of the Andaman Islands, whereas Barren Island is in the northern Andaman Sea. Statement 3 is incorrect: The Barren Island volcano has erupted multiple times after 1991. Significant eruptions were recorded in 1994–1995, 2005, 2009, and most recently in 2017–2018. Thus, it has not remained inactive since 1991.

The Aral Sea is a salt lake in Central Asia which was previously the fourth-largest salt lake in the world. It started to shrink in the 1960s as a result of the Soviet Union starting to divert water for agricultural use. According to NASA, water diversion for agricultural purposes in the region has caused the Aral Sea to lose nearly 75% of its original size during the past 50 years. The Aral Sea’s depth has similarly dropped, going from 68 metres in the 1960s to less than 10 meters now.

subject: International Relations
stem: In the Indian context, what is the implication of ratifying the ‘Additional Protocol’ with the ‘International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)’?
options: The civilian nuclear reactors come under IAEA safeguards.
The military nuclear installations come under the inspection of IAEA.
The country will have the privilege to buy uranium from the Nuclear Suppliers Group (NSG).
The country automatically becomes a member of the NSG.

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: The civilian nuclear reactors come under IAEA safeguards.
solution.detail: The Additional Protocol (AP) is a legal agreement between a country and the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) that strengthens the IAEA’s ability to verify that nuclear materials are not being diverted for non-peaceful purposes. It expands the IAEA’s access to nuclear facilities and provides additional transparency measures. India signed the Additional Protocol with the IAEA in 2009, and it came into force in 2014. Option (a) is correct: The Additional Protocol strengthens IAEA oversight over India’s civilian nuclear reactors, ensuring non-diversion of nuclear material for military purposes. India voluntarily placed 14 civilian reactors under IAEA safeguards as part of the Indo-US Nuclear Deal (2008). Option (b) is incorrect: India’s military nuclear installations are not subject to IAEA safeguards as India is not a signatory to the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT). Option (c) is incorrect: While ratifying the protocol demonstrates India’s commitment to non-proliferation but it does not directly grant the privilege to buy uranium from NSG countries. However, it played a role in securing an NSG waiver in 2008 which allowed India to access nuclear technology and materials despite not being an NPT signatory Option (d) is incorrect: Ratifying the Additional Protocol does not automatically make India a member of the Nuclear Suppliers Group (NSG). NSG membership requires a consensus among all member states, and India’s membership has faced opposition due to its non-signatory status to the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT).

solution.extraEdge:
The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), established in 1957 and headquartered in Vienna, Austria, is an independent organization within the UN system. It promotes the safe, secure, and peaceful use of nuclear energy while preventing its misuse for military purposes. The IAEA focuses on three main areas:
- Nuclear safety and security
- Science and technology
- Safeguards and verification It assists member states with nuclear research, ensures promotion of Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT), and fosters international cooperation. The agency was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2005 for its efforts in promoting nuclear safety and non-proliferation.

subject: International Relations
stem: International Labour Organization’s Conventions 138 and 182 are related to:
options: Adaptation of agricultural practices to global climate change
Regulation of food prices and food security

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: Child labour
solution.detail:
The International Labour Organization (ILO) has two key conventions focused on child labour:
- ILO Convention 138 (Minimum Age Convention, 1973) focuses on the abolition of child labour by setting a minimum age for employment or work, which should not be less than the age of completion of compulsory schooling and, in any case, for most of the countries, not less than 15 years. It aims to ensure that children are not employed in work that jeopardizes their health, safety, or morals.
- ILO Convention 182 (Worst Forms of Child Labour Convention, 1999) aims to eliminate the worst forms of child labour, including slavery, trafficking, debt bondage, forced labour, and hazardous work. It is recognized as one of the core ILO conventions, widely ratified by countries worldwide.

subject: International Relations
stem: “Rule of Law Index” is released by which of the following ?
options: The Office of UN Commissioner for Human Rights

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: World Justice Project
solution.detail:
The “Rule of Law Index” is an annual report published by the World Justice Project (WJP). It evaluates countries based on their adherence to the rule of law using factors such as:
- Constraints on government powers
- Absence of corruption
- Open government
- Fundamental rights
- Order and security
- Regulatory enforcement
- Civil and criminal justice The index ranks countries based on data collected from experts and surveys to assess how well nations uphold legal principles. The index is widely used by governments, policymakers, and civil society organizations to identify strengths and weaknesses in the rule of law and to guide reforms.

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: 1 only
solution.detail: Statement 1 is correct: NSG members can engage in nuclear trade and obtain access to advanced nuclear technology and materials. This includes technologies for peaceful uses such as medicine, energy production, and research. India seeks NSG membership to improve its access to civilian nuclear technology and fuel for energy generation. Statement 2 is incorrect: NSG membership does not automatically grant NPT membership. Adherence to NPT principles is often considered during NSG membership discussions but signing the NPT is not a mandatory requirement for joining the NSG. For instance, India seeks NSG membership without being an NPT signatory. China has blocked India’s entry into NSG by citing India’s non-signatory status in NPT.

subject: International Relations
stem: Very recently, in which of the following countries have lakhs of people either suffered from severe famine/acute malnutrition or died due to starvation caused by war/ethnic conflicts?

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: Yemen and South Sudan
solution.detail:
Yemen and South Sudan have experienced severe humanitarian crises in recent years, with millions of people suffering from famine, acute malnutrition, and starvation, primarily caused by ongoing wars and ethnic conflicts.
- Yemen has been experiencing one of the world’s worst humanitarian crises due to a prolonged conflict since 2015. The war, combined with a blockade, has led to widespread famine-like conditions, with millions of people suffering from acute malnutrition and starvation. Over 80% of the population requires humanitarian aid, and millions are at risk of famine.
- Since its civil war began in 2013, South Sudan has faced severe food insecurity and famine conditions. Conflict has displaced millions, disrupted agricultural activities, and caused widespread hunger. In 2017, a famine was officially declared in parts of South Sudan, and even now, half the population faces extreme hunger.

This extinction event is primarily driven by human activities such as over-exploitation of resources, habitat destruction, pollution, and climate change.

Option (c) is correct: Wanna Cry (2017) and Petya (2016) are types of ransomware attacks that exploited vulnerabilities in Microsoft Windows, with EternalBlue being the exploit used in these attacks. These cyber attacks caused widespread disruption across businesses and governments globally by encrypting data and demanding ransom. Options (a), (b) and (d) are incorrect:

- Mini satellites are small satellites used in space technology, and are unrelated to these cybersecurity threats.

solution.extraEdge:
A cyberattack is a malicious and deliberate attempt by an individual or organization to breach another individual’s information system. Some of the most common cyber attacks are:
- Phishing: Phishing is the practice of sending fraudulent communications that appear to come from a reputable source, usually through email.
- Man-in-the-middle (MitM) attacks: MitM attacks, also known as eavesdropping attacks, occur when attackers insert themselves into a two-party transaction.
- Denial-of-service attack: A denial-of-service attack floods systems, servers, or networks with traffic to exhaust resources and bandwidth.
- SQL injection: A Structured Query Language (SQL) injection occurs when an attacker inserts malicious code into a server that uses SQL and forces the server to reveal information it normally would not.
- Zero-day exploit: A zero-day exploit hits after a network vulnerability is announced but before a patch or solution is implemented. Attackers target the disclosed vulnerability during this window of time.

Option (a) is incorrect: An Israeli radar system refers to systems like the Iron Dome, which is developed by Israel for intercepting short-range threats, but THAAD is an American system. Option (b) is incorrect: India’s indigenous anti-missile programme refers to systems like the Aakash missile system, but THAAD is a U.S.-developed system, not an Indian one. It works by using powerful radar systems to detect and track incoming missiles and then using interceptors to destroy them mid-flight. Option (d) is incorrect: A defence collaboration between Japan and South Korea refers to missile defense collaborations, but THAAD is specifically an American-developed system, not a bilateral project between Japan and South Korea.

subject: International Relations
stem: Consider the following events : 1. The first democratically elected communist party government formed in a State in India. 2. India’s then largest bank, ‘Imperial Bank of India’, was renamed ‘State Bank of India’. 3. Air India was nationalised and became the national carrier. 4. Goa became a part of independent India. Which of the following is the correct chronological sequence of the above events ?

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: 3-2-1-4
solution.detail: Air India was nationalized in 1953 under the Air Corporations Act, 1953. Imperial Bank of India was renamed as the State Bank of India (SBI) in 1955 as part of banking sector reforms. India’s first democratically elected communist government was formed in Kerala in 1957, with E.M.S. Namboodiripad as Chief Minister. Goa was annexed by India and became part of independent India in 1961 after “Operation Vijay”. Therefore, the correct chronological sequence is 3-2-1-4. Thus, the correct chronological order is 1953 (Air India Nationalization) → 1955 (SBI Renaming) → 1957 (First Communist Government) → 1961 (Goa’s Liberation).

The NFSA covers up to 75% of the rural population and 50% of the urban population, not just BPL families. It includes Priority Households and Antyodaya Anna Yojana (AAY) beneficiaries.
As per Section 13 of the NFSA, the eldest woman (18 years or above) is mandated to be the head of the household for the purpose of ration card issuance.
Pregnant women and lactating mothers receive free meals under the Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS) scheme, but no fixed calorie intake of 1600 kcal per day is prescribed in the NFSA.

subject: International Relations
stem: The identity platform ‘Aadhaar’ provides open “Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)”. What does it imply? 1. It can be integrated into any electronic device. 2. Online authentication using iris is possible. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: Both 1 and 2
solution.detail: Statement 1 is correct: Aadhaar APIs can be integrated into multiple electronic devices such as biometric scanners, mobile phones, and ATMs to enable identity verification and digital transactions. Statement 2 is correct: Aadhaar authentication supports irisbased verification, which is more secure and accurate than fingerprint-based authentication, especially for elderly individuals and those engaged in manual labor. The Aadhaar identity platform enables seamless authentication and integration with various applications through open Application Programming Interfaces (APIs), facilitating secure digital identity verification.

subject: International Relations
stem: Consider the following statements: 1. Aadhaar card can be used as a proof of citizenship or domicile. 2. Once issued, Aadhaar number cannot be deactivated or omit- ted by the Issuing Authority. Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: Neither 1 nor 2
solution.detail: Statement 1 is incorrect: Aadhaar is a 12-digit unique identification number issued by UIDAI to Indian residents based on biometric and demographic data. However, it does not serve as proof of citizenship or domicile, as it is issued to all residents, including foreign nationals residing in India. Statement 2 is incorrect: Aadhaar numbers can be deactivated or omitted by UIDAI in cases of fraud, duplication, or non-use for an extended period, as per Section 23 of the Aadhaar Act, 2016.

subject: International Relations
stem: As per the NSSO 70th Round “Situation Assessment Survey of Agricultural Households” Consider the following statements: 1. Rajasthan has the highest percentage share of agricultural households among its rural households. 2. Out of the total agricultural households in the country, a little over 60 percent belong to OBCs. 3. In Kerala, a little over 60 percent of agricultural households re- ported to have received maximum income from sources other than agricultural activities. Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: 1 and 3 only
solution.detail: The NSSO 70th Round (2012-13) “Situation Assessment Survey of Agricultural Households” provides crucial data on the socio-economic conditions of Indian farmers. Statement 1 is correct: Rajasthan had the highest percentage of agricultural households (78.4 percent) among its rural households followed by Uttar Pradesh (74.8 percent) and Madhya Pradesh (70.8 percent). Statement 2 is incorrect: As per the survey, about 45 percent out of the total agricultural households in the country belonged to Other Backward Classes (OBC). About 16% of agricultural households were from Scheduled Castes (SC) and 13 percent were from Scheduled Tribes (ST). Statement 3 is correct: In Kerala, over 60% of agricultural households derive their main income from non-agricultural sources such as services, remittances, and trade due to the state’s economic structure.

subject: Medieval History
stem: Which one of the following foreign travelers elaborately discussed diamonds and diamond mines of India?

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: Jean-Baptiste Tavernier
solution.detail: Jean-Baptiste Tavernier was a French traveler and gem merchant who traveled extensively in India during the 17th century. He was an independent traveller and trader who, between the years of 1630 and 1668, made travels to Persia and India in the courts of Shah Jahan and Aurangzeb. He is wellknown for his detailed accounts of the diamonds and diamond mines of India, particularly the famous Golconda mines. Tavernier provided extensive information on the diamond trade, the mines in places like Golconda, and the prized diamonds, such as the Koh-i-Noor.

subject: Modern History
stem: Which one of the following statements does not apply to the system of Subsidiary Alliance introduced by Lord Wellesley?
options: To maintain a large standing army at others expense
To keep India safe from Napoleonic danger
To secure a fixed income for the Company
To establish British paramountcy over the Indian States

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: To secure a fixed income for the Company
solution.detail: The Subsidiary Alliance was a diplomatic strategy introduced by Lord Wellesley in 1798, aiming to maintain British control over Indian states. Option (c) is correct: The Subsidiary Alliance aimed to establish British paramountcy over the Indian States and to ensure their military and political subservience to the British East India Company. Securing a fixed income for the company was not the primary objective of the system. Hyderabad was the first state to come under it. The native Indian ruler had to pay for the maintenance of the British army and if he failed to make the payment, a portion of his territory was taken away and ceded to the British. They could not engage in alliances with other powers without British consent.

subject: Modern History
stem: After the Santhal Uprising subsided, what was/ were the measure/measures taken by the colonial government? 1. The territories called ‘Santhal Paraganas’ were created. 2. It became illegal for a Santhal to transfer land to a non-Santhal. Select the correct answer using the code given below:

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: Both 1 and 2
solution.detail: The Santhal Rebellion (1855-56) in the Rajmahal Hills was sparked by the oppression of revenue officials, police, moneylenders, and landlords (referred to as “dikus” by the Santhals). Led by Sido and Kanhu, the Santhals revolted, declaring independence from the British. The rebellion was suppressed by 1856 after extensive military action. Statement (1) is correct: After the Santhal Uprising (1855– 56), the British created the Santhal Parganas in 1855 by carving out areas from Bhagalpur and Birbhum districts to provide the Santhals with a separate administrative region. Statement (2) is correct: The British introduced legal restrictions on land transfers, making it illegal for a Santhal to transfer land to a non-Santhal. This was done to prevent exploitation by moneylenders (Mahajans) and landlords (Zamindars), which had been a major cause of the rebellion.

subject: Modern History
stem: Which one of the following is a very significant aspect of the Champaran Satyagraha?
options: Active all-India participation of lawyers, students and women in the National Movement
Active involvement of Dalit and Tribal communities of India in the National Movement
Joining of peasant unrest to India’s National Movement
Drastic decrease in the cultivation of plantation crops and commercial crops

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: Joining of peasant unrest to India’s National Movement
solution.detail: Champaran Satyagraha (1917) was undertaken after Mahatma Gandhi learned about the abuses suffered by farmers, who were forced into growing indigo by British planters and estate owners. Under the Tinkathia system in Champaran, tenants were forced to grow indigo on 3/20th of their land for landlords. European planters imposed high rents and illegal fees, sparking passive resistance. This marked the beginning of Gandhian methods and the involvement of peasants in the Indian National Movement.

subject: Modern History
stem: In 1920, which of the following changed its name to “Swarajya Sabha”?

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: All India Home Rule League
solution.detail: In 1920, the All India Home Rule League, founded by Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Annie Besant, changed its name to the Swarajya Sabha. This was in line with the growing demand for complete self-rule (Swarajya) and the influence of the Non-Cooperation Movement led by Mahatma Gandhi. It aimed to promote the idea of “Home Rule” and played a key role in mobilizing public opinion, laying the groundwork for India’s struggle for independence.

stem: In the Federation established by the Government of India Act of 1935, residuary powers were given to the:

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: Governor General
solution.detail: The Government of India Act, 1935 divided powers between the Centre and provinces into three lists: Federal (59 items), Provincial (54 items), and Concurrent (36 items). It granted residuary powers to the Governor-General, who could authorize either legislature to legislate on such matters.

subject: Modern History
stem: The staple commodities of export by the English East Indian Company from Bengal in the middle of the 18th century were
options: Copper, silver, gold, spices and tea

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: Cotton, silk, saltpeter and opium
solution.detail: The East India Company was originally formed in Britain for pursuing trade with the East Indies in Southeast Asia. It ended up trading mainly with the Indian subcontinent and China, where the main items of trade were cotton, silk, tea and opium. Also Bengal produced saltpeter, a key ingredient for making gunpowder, which was in high demand for both domestic use and export.

subject: Modern History
stem: Regarding Wood’s Dispatch, which of the following statements are true? 1. Grants-in-Aid system was introduced. 2. Establishment of universities was recommended. 3. English as a medium of instruction at all levels of education was recommended. Select the correct answer using the code given below:

solution.verdict: Option A is correct: 1 and 2 only
solution.detail: Wood’s Dispatch (1854) is known as the Magna Carta of English Education in India. Under this government was to take up the responsibility for the Education of masses. It provided for hierarchy of Educational Institutions i.e. three tier education system. It also emphasised on Female, Vocational Education and teachers’ training. The Dispatch proposed secular education in Government Institutions. Statement 1 is correct: The Despatch advocated for the introduction of a Grants-in-Aid system to encourage private and missionary enterprises in education. Under this scheme, financial assistance was provided to educational institutions that met certain standards thereby promoting the expansion and improvement of education. Statement 2 is correct: It systematised the hierarchy from vernacular primary schools in villages at bottom, followed by Anglo-Vernacular High Schools and an affiliated college at the district level and affiliating universities in the presidency towns of Calcutta, Bombay and Madras. Statement 3 is incorrect: The Woods Dispatch recommended English as the Medium of Instruction in higher studies and vernaculars language in at school level.

statements.text: The Despatch advocated for the introduction of a Grants-in-Aid system to encourage private and missionary enterprises in education. Under this scheme, financial assistance was provided to educational institutions that met certain standards thereby promoting the expansion and improvement of education.

It systematised the hierarchy from vernacular primary schools in villages at bottom, followed by Anglo-Vernacular High Schools and an affiliated college at the district level and affiliating universities in the presidency towns of Calcutta, Bombay and Madras.
The Woods Dispatch recommended English as the Medium of Instruction in higher studies and vernaculars language in at school level.

solution.trick: S3 overreaches in its claim — while English may have been promoted at the higher levels of education to train clerks and administrators, recommending it as the medium of instruction “at all levels” is logically flawed and pedagogically impractical. For primary and vernacular schooling, such a policy would have been both unfeasible and counterproductive, especially in rural colonial India. Hence likely false.

subject: Modern History
stem: Which of the following led to the introduction of English Education in India? 1. Charter Act of 1813 2. General Committee of Public Instruction, 1823 3. Orientalist and Anglicist Controversy. Select the correct answer using the code given below:

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: 1, 2 and 3
solution.detail: Statement 1 is correct: The idea of supporting educated Indians and promoting knowledge of contemporary sciences in the country was enshrined into the Charter Act of 1813. The Charter Act of 1813 permitted Christian missionaries to propagate English and preach their religion. It mandated the company to allocate one lakh rupees annually for the revival and improvement of literature and the promotion of science among the inhabitants of British India. The Act emphasized the need to educate Indians in Western sciences and literature which indirectly laid the foundation for English education. Statement 2 is correct: A “General Committee of Public Instruction” was established by the Governor-General-in- Council in 1823 with the task of allocating one lakh rupees for educational purposes under the Presidentship of Lord Macaulay. Lord Macaulay’s Minute resolved the debate in favor of the Anglicists, directing the limited government resources toward the teaching of Western sciences and literature through the medium of the English language. Statement 3 is correct: The Orientalist-Anglicist Controversy in the early 19th century centered on the medium and content of education in India. Orientalists led by scholars like H.T. Prinsep advocated for preserving traditional Indian knowledge through Sanskrit, Persian, and Arabic. Anglicists led by Thomas Babington Macaulay, pushed for English education to create a class of Indians who were culturally English but Indian by birth. The debate culminated in Macaulay's Minute of 1835 which favored English education. Governor-General Lord William Bentinck endorsed this view thereby officially making English the medium of instruction in higher education.

subject: Modern History
stem: With reference to educational institutions during colonial rule in India, consider the following pairs: Institution Founder 1. Sanskrit College at Benaras - William Jones 2. Calcutta Madrasa - Warren Hastings 3. Fort William College - Arthur Wellesley Which of the pairs given above is/are correct?

solution.verdict: Option B is correct: 2 only
solution.detail: Pair 1 is incorrectly matched: The Sanskrit College at Benaras was founded in 1791 by Jonathan Duncan (not William Jones). The objective was to promote the study of Hindu law and philosophy among the people of Benaras., Sir William Jones was a renowned philologist and scholar who founded the Asiatic Society in 1784 in Calcutta, aiming to encourage Oriental studies. Pair 2 is correctly matched: The Calcutta Madrasah was established by Warren Hastings in 1781 for the study of Muslim law and related subjects to promote the study of Islamic law and jurisprudence among the Muslim population. Pair 3 is incorrectly matched: Fort William College was set up by Lord Richard Wellesley in 1800 for training of civil servants of the Company in languages and customs of Indians.

subject: Modern History
stem: Who among the following were the founders of the "Hind Mazdoor Sabha" established in 1948 ?
options: B. Krishna Pillai, E.M.S. Namboodiripad and K.C. George
Jayaprakash Narayan, Deen Dayal Upadhyay and M.N. Roy
C.P. Ramaswamy Iyer, K. Kamaraj and Veeresalingam Pantulu
Ashok Mehta, T.S. Ramanujam and G.G. Mehta

solution.verdict: Option D is correct: Ashok Mehta, T.S. Ramanujam and G.G. Mehta
solution.detail: The Hind Mazdoor Sabha (HMS), established in 1948, is one of the prominent trade union organizations in India. Founded by notable leaders such as Ashok Mehta, T.S. Ramanujam, and G.G. Mehta, HMS aimed to represent the interests of Indian workers and promote their rights and welfare. The organization played a vital role in advocating for labor reforms, improving working conditions, and securing fair wages for workers across various industries.
solution.extraEdge:
Other prominent trade unions in India were:
- All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC): Founded in 1920 by Lala Lajpat Rai and others, AITUC is one of the oldest trade unions in India and is affiliated with the Communist Party of India (CPI).
- Indian National Trade Union Congress (INTUC): Established in 1947 by Sardar Patel, INTUC is associated with the Indian National Congress (INC).
- Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh (BMS): Founded in 1955 by Dattopant Thengdi, BMS is linked to the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS) and focuses on the rights and welfare of workers.
- Centre of Indian Trade Unions (CITU): Formed in 1970 by E.M.S. Namboodiripad, P. Sundarayya, and Harkishan Singh Surjeet, CITU is affiliated with the Communist Party of India (Marxist) (CPI-M).
- Self-Employed Women's Association (SEWA): Established in 1972 by Ela Bhatt, SEWA is based in Ahmedabad and focuses on the rights of self-employed women, providing them with support and advocacy.

subject: Modern History
stem: He wrote biographies of Mazzini, Garibaldi, Shivaji and Shrikrishna; stayed in America for some time, and was also elected to the Central Assembly. He was:

solution.verdict: Option C is correct: Lala Lajpat Rai
solution.detail: Lala Lajpat Rai was popularly known as Punjab Kesari. He was the INC’s president at the 1920 Calcutta Special Session. In 1921, he founded Servants of the People Society, a non-profit welfare organisation, in Lahore. He also wrote biographies of Mazzini, Garibaldi, Shivaji, and Shrikrishna. He led a nonviolent march in protest against the Simon Commission and was brutally assaulted by the police. He succumbed to a heart attack later, with the assault likely contributing to his weakened condition.

solution.detail: Pair 1 is correctly matched: All India Liberal Federation was founded by Surendra Nath Banarjea and some of its prominent leaders were Tej Bahadur Sapru, V. S. Srinivasa Sastri and M. R. Jayakar. Tej Bahadur Sapru was one of the presidents of Indian National Liberal Federation. It advocated for gradual constitutional progress and dominion status for India. Pair 2 is correctly matched: K.C. Neogy was a member of the Constituent Assembly of India, a member of the first Cabinet of independent India and the chairman of the first Finance Commission of India.
